items.title: Fair coin vs biased coin
items.scenario: Alice flips a coin each morning to decide whether to take the bus (heads) or bike (T). A fair coin gives P(H) = P(T) = 0.5; later she switches to a coin with P(H) = 0.9.
items.explanation: Fair coin: H = 1 bit—each flip resolves one bit of uncertainty. Biased coin: H ≈ −0.9 log₂ 0.9 − 0.1 log₂ 0.1 ≈ 0.47 bits. The outcome is usually predictable (bus), so average surprise drops even though both outcomes remain possible.

items.title: Customer support ticket categories
items.scenario: A help desk receives tickets labeled billing (50%), login (30%), feature request (15%), or other (5%). Leadership wants to know how many bits of uncertainty a random ticket label carries.
items.explanation: H(X) = −Σ p log₂ p ≈ 1.88 bits. Billing-heavy skew lowers entropy compared to four equally likely categories (which would be 2 bits). This quantifies how informative a single label is before reading the ticket body.

items.title: Decision tree split on a binary feature
items.scenario: A churn model has root node labels {stay, leave} with 50/50 counts. Feature "uses mobile app" splits the node into two pure child nodes: app users always stay, non-users always leave.
items.explanation: Root entropy H = 1 bit (maximum for two classes). After the split, each child has H = 0 (deterministic). **Information gain** = 1 − 0 = 1 bit—the feature eliminated all label uncertainty. Entropy reduction guides which split to choose.

items.title: Language model perplexity
items.scenario: A next-token model on a 50k vocabulary achieves average cross-entropy 4.6 nats per token on a held-out corpus.
items.explanation: Perplexity ≈ exp(4.6) ≈ 99 effective choices per step. Lower cross-entropy (and thus lower entropy of the model's implied distribution relative to data) means the model is less surprised by real text—entropy-related quantities summarize prediction quality.
